weather.tip: A cool, overcast 20.6°C start with next to no rain risk (2%) today, so no need for an umbrella on the commute in. It'll climb to a warm 32.5°C by afternoon, so carry water if you're out past midday.

around.headline: Two newly married women die by suicide in Bengaluru in separate cases
around.summary: Two women died by suicide in separate incidents days after their weddings, with one case involving a note alleging the husband delayed visiting her; both point to strain in early marriages. If you notice a newly married friend or relative withdrawing or seeming afraid, check in directly rather than assuming it's normal adjustment stress.
around.source: The Times of India / India Today / Deccan Herald

around.headline: Flipkart delivery agent arrested for barging into Bengaluru woman's home and exposing himself
around.summary: Police have arrested the delivery executive who pushed his way into a woman's flat and exposed himself after she opened the door; Flipkart has separately terminated him. Keep the door on the chain and verify a delivery person through the app before opening up fully, especially if you're home alone.

around.headline: BMTC conductor suspended, crews warned after transport minister denied a seat over exact change
around.summary: A conductor was suspended after asking a passenger — who turned out to be Karnataka's transport minister, riding incognito — to get off the bus over not having exact change; BMTC has now formally warned crews against this practice. Carry the Namma BMTC app or a UPI QR handy so change disputes don't hold up your ride.
around.source: The Times of India / NDTV / The Economic Times

around.headline: BMTC launches its longest-ever bus route, a 101-km run to Electronic City
around.summary: BMTC has rolled out its longest single route yet, spanning 101 km to Electronic City, aimed at easing the commute for tech-corridor workers. Worth checking if it cuts your travel time versus your current combination of buses or cabs.

around.headline: Karnataka CM outlines 500-km Bengaluru Metro expansion; Home Minister calls for tech-led traffic fixes
around.summary: Karnataka's Chief Minister has laid out a 500-km expansion plan for Bengaluru Metro, while Home Minister Priyank Kharge has separately called for technology-driven solutions to ease the city's traffic congestion. Expect years of construction-related disruption around new metro corridors as this rolls out.

around.headline: Indian sailor killed as Iranian missiles strike oil tankers in Strait of Hormuz amid US-Iran conflict
around.summary: One Indian sailor was killed and six wounded after Iranian missiles hit oil tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, as the US-Iran conflict escalates and Trump floats a 20% fee on cargo through the strait. The strait carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil, so a prolonged standoff there tends to push petrol and diesel prices higher at home.

events.note: A celebration of language, rhythm and storytelling — a good pick if you're into spoken word or literary events.

events.note: A themed Awadhi cuisine spread for anyone looking to try something different for a meal out.

events.when: Kicks off this week, check venue for match schedule
events.note: A hockey tournament in the city — worth catching live if you follow the sport.

events.note: A food festival with appearances from well-known chefs and personalities.

events.note: A centenary celebration of the artist's work — a good stop for anyone into art exhibitions.

street_smart.title: Your phone is snatched by a biker who speeds off. What do you do first?
street_smart.based_on: Evergreen rotation topic (phone snatching response / CEIR blocking) — no single matching incident in today's feed, and this week's Bengaluru safety news (dowry-related deaths, the delivery-agent home intrusion) was already the basis for street-smart scenarios in the last few days.
street_smart.situation: You're walking near a signal when a biker grabs your phone out of your hand and speeds off before you can react. Your banking apps, UPI, email and OTPs are all tied to that number and device. What's your first move?

choices.text: Chase the biker on foot or jump on your own two-wheeler to try to catch him and get the phone back.
choices.feedback: Chasing a fleeing biker in traffic is a real injury risk for a device that's replaceable, and the odds of catching him are low. Worse, every minute spent chasing is a minute your SIM and apps stay live and exploitable — the clock that actually matters is the one on your accounts, not the one on the biker.

choices.text: Call your bank and freeze your UPI/banking apps first, but leave blocking the SIM card for later since the phone itself is already gone.
choices.feedback: Freezing banking apps helps, but leaving the SIM active means the thief can still receive OTPs and password-reset links for every other account tied to that number — email, social media, food and cab apps with saved cards. The SIM block should happen in the same breath as the banking freeze, not after.

choices.text: Change your important passwords from a friend's phone, and plan to visit the police station and block the SIM/IMEI the next day.
choices.feedback: Changing passwords is the right instinct, but waiting until 'tomorrow' to block the SIM and IMEI hands the thief a full day of a live, usable device and number. These steps take minutes over a phone call or a website — there's no good reason to delay them to the next day.

choices.text: Immediately call your telecom operator's helpline to block the SIM, block the phone's IMEI via ceir.gov.in, and file a police complaint — all within the hour.
choices.feedback: Correct. Blocking the SIM stops OTP interception immediately, blocking the IMEI (India's Central Equipment Identity Register) makes the handset useless nationwide even if resold, and a police complaint creates the paper trail insurers and banks may later ask for. Doing all three within the hour closes the window attackers actually rely on.

takeaways: Block your SIM the moment your phone is gone — call your operator's helpline from any other phone, or use their website/app.
Block the IMEI nationwide at ceir.gov.in; dial *#06# on any phone in advance and save the IMEI somewhere off-device so you have it ready if this ever happens.
File a police complaint or e-FIR and change passwords for banking, email and UPI from another device — do all of this within the same hour, not the next day.

quiz.q: A BMTC conductor was suspended this week after asking a passenger to get off a bus over lack of exact change. Who turned out to be that passenger?
options: A random commuter who complained on social media
A journalist doing a sting operation
quiz.why: The minister's incognito bus ride was meant to check on commuter issues firsthand; BMTC has since formally warned crews against denying rides over change, and apps or UPI QR codes on board can sidestep the dispute entirely.

quiz.q: What triggered the death of an Indian sailor and injuries to six others in the Strait of Hormuz this week?
options: Iranian missiles struck oil tankers amid the US-Iran conflict
A collision with a cargo ship
quiz.why: The strait is a chokepoint for roughly a fifth of the world's oil shipments, so escalation there tends to ripple into fuel prices well beyond the region, including in India.

quiz.q: Karnataka's Chief Minister this week outlined an expansion plan for Bengaluru Metro spanning how many kilometres?
quiz.why: The plan comes alongside a separate push from the state's Home Minister for technology-driven fixes to Bengaluru's traffic congestion, reflecting how much of the city's near-term infrastructure spending is aimed at transit.

quiz.q: If your phone is stolen in India, which government portal lets you block the handset nationwide by its IMEI number, making it unusable even if resold?
quiz.why: CEIR stands for Central Equipment Identity Register — it blocks a device across every telecom operator in the country, not just your own, so it works even if the thief switches SIMs.

quiz.q: Which constitutional body is primarily responsible for conducting elections in India?
quiz.why: The Election Commission of India is an independent constitutional body under Article 324, distinct from bodies like the UPSC (recruitment exams) or Finance Commission (centre-state revenue sharing).
